text: As established in the preceding chapters, GCC governments have rapidly developed robust national systems of public higher education. Alongside a growing array of private institutions, these initiatives have significantly expanded academic access for a diverse range of students. This increased local capacity has been especially transformative for women, who can now pursue degrees at home—a vital shift from previous generations when cultural barriers often prevented daughters from studying abroad. Furthermore, the expansion of regional universities has created new opportunities for the children of expatriate workers, allowing them to complete their higher education within the GCC rather than being forced to relocate to their countries of origin.
